---
title: "Choosing a Demeaner Backend"
description: "How to pick the right fixed-effects demeaner for your problem."
categories: [performance, fixed effects]
jupyter: python3
---

PyFixest exposes several demeaner backends via the `demeaner=` argument.
All backends produce numerically equivalent results; the choice only affects **speed**.

For a deep dive on *why* some fixed-effects problems are harder than others — and detailed
benchmarks comparing all backends — see
[When Are Fixed Effects Estimations Difficult?](../explanation/difficult-fixed-effects.md).

```{python}
import pyfixest as pf
from pyfixest.demeaners import LsmrDemeaner, MapDemeaner, WithinDemeaner

df = pf.get_data()
```

## Quick Reference

| Backend | When to use |
|---|---|
| `MapDemeaner(backend="rust")` | **Default.** Dense fixed effects / well-connected graphs. No extra dependencies. |
| `MapDemeaner(backend="numba")` | Alternative MAP implementation. Requires `pip install pyfixest[numba]`. |
| `WithinDemeaner()` | Best for **sparse** fixed-effect structures (e.g. matched employer-employee data). |
| `MapDemeaner(backend="jax")` | MAP on the GPU via JAX. Requires `pip install pyfixest[jax]`. |
| `LsmrDemeaner(backend="cupy", device="cuda")` | LSMR solver on CUDA via CuPy. |
| `LsmrDemeaner(backend="cupy", device="cpu")` | LSMR solver on CPU via SciPy. |
| `LsmrDemeaner(backend="torch", device="cuda")` | GPU acceleration via PyTorch. Experimental. |

## `MapDemeaner` — Method of Alternating Projections

The default demeaner. It iteratively subtracts each fixed effect's group means
in turn until convergence — the classic Gauss-Seidel / "ZigZag" algorithm.

**When to use:** dense, well-connected fixed-effect graphs (e.g. a standard two-way
firm + worker FE where many workers move across many firms). For sparse or weakly-connected
graphs, use `WithinDemeaner` instead.

```{python}
# Default (Rust MAP)
fit = pf.feols("Y ~ X1 | f1", data=df)

# Rust backend – explicit
fit = pf.feols("Y ~ X1 | f1", data=df, demeaner=MapDemeaner(backend="rust"))

# Numba backend (requires pip install pyfixest[numba])
fit = pf.feols("Y ~ X1 | f1", data=df, demeaner=MapDemeaner(backend="numba"))

# Tighter convergence tolerance
fit = pf.feols(
    "Y ~ X1 | f1",
    data=df,
    demeaner=MapDemeaner(backend="rust", fixef_tol=1e-10, fixef_maxiter=50_000),
)
```

## `WithinDemeaner` — Krylov-Schwarz Solver

Solves the demeaning problem as a sparse linear system using a Krylov method
(CG or GMRES) with an additive or multiplicative Schwarz preconditioner.
Implemented in Rust via the [`within`](https://github.com/py-econometrics/within) library.

**When to use:** sparse or weakly-connected fixed-effect structures — for example
matched employer-employee panels where each worker appears in only a handful of firms.
In these cases `MapDemeaner` can converge very slowly, while `WithinDemeaner` often needs
far fewer iterations.

```{python}
# CG + additive Schwarz (default)
fit = pf.feols("Y ~ X1 | f1 + f2", data=df, demeaner=WithinDemeaner())

# GMRES + additive Schwarz
fit = pf.feols(
    "Y ~ X1 | f1 + f2",
    data=df,
    demeaner=WithinDemeaner(krylov="gmres", preconditioner="additive", gmres_restart=30),
)

# GMRES + multiplicative Schwarz — stronger preconditioner for very sparse graphs
fit = pf.feols(
    "Y ~ X1 | f1 + f2",
    data=df,
    demeaner=WithinDemeaner(
        krylov="gmres",
        preconditioner="multiplicative",
        gmres_restart=50,
    ),
)

# Unpreconditioned CG — useful for debugging
fit = pf.feols(
    "Y ~ X1 | f1 + f2", data=df, demeaner=WithinDemeaner(krylov="cg", preconditioner="off")
)
```

The `preconditioner` argument accepts `"additive"` (default), `"multiplicative"`, and `"off"`.
Note that `"multiplicative"` requires `krylov="gmres"`.

## `LsmrDemeaner` — Sparse LSMR

Instead of group-ID arithmetic, this backend builds the sparse dummy-variable design matrix
for all fixed effects and solves via LSMR (Fong & Saunders, 2011).
Two sub-backends are available: `"cupy"` (CuPy/SciPy) and `"torch"` (experimental PyTorch).

**When to use:** GPU-accelerated workloads. On CPU, `WithinDemeaner` is generally faster.

```{python}
# SciPy on CPU (always available, no GPU required)
fit = pf.feols(
    "Y ~ X1 | f1 + f2",
    data=df,
    demeaner=LsmrDemeaner(backend="cupy", device="cpu"),
)
```

LSMR uses two tolerance parameters instead of one:

- `fixef_atol` — gradient-norm stopping criterion (default `1e-8`)
- `fixef_btol` — residual-norm stopping criterion (default `1e-8`)

```{python}
# Tight tolerances
fit = pf.feols(
    "Y ~ X1 | f1",
    data=df,
    demeaner=LsmrDemeaner(backend="cupy", device="cpu", fixef_atol=1e-10, fixef_btol=1e-10),
)
```

## Benchmarks

The figures below show median runtimes for the "simple" (dense, well-connected) and
"difficult" (sparse, weakly-connected) DGPs from the
[fixest benchmarks](https://github.com/kylebutts/fixest_benchmarks), with `k=10` covariates.

**Simple DGP** — dense graph, both CPU backends competitive:

![Simple DGP benchmark](../explanation/figures/base-benchmarks/bench_simple.png){width=90%}

**Difficult DGP** — sparse graph, vanilla MAP degrades sharply:

![Difficult DGP benchmark](../explanation/figures/base-benchmarks/bench_difficult.png){width=90%}

For the full benchmark suite including AKM employer-employee panels with varying
mobility and sorting, see
[When Are Fixed Effects Estimations Difficult?](../explanation/difficult-fixed-effects.md).
